capability_objective: I need a grounded comparison for an audit note. Search separately for “Vision screening — Erin Brooks” in North Clinic and “Nutrition consult — Sam Adeyemi” in South Clinic, issuing those independent searches together in one assistant action. Only after each search returns a unique ID, retrieve both complete appointment records together in a later action and compare their status and date. Do not update, cancel, or notify either record. If either exact search returns zero or multiple matches, stop that branch and ask me to disambiguate.
prompt:
Prepare a grounded, read-only appointment comparison in this sandbox. Use Pi's genuine Bash tool to run the local `./appointmentctl` executable for every appointment-system operation. Begin by running `./appointmentctl --help` exactly once; that output describes all operation arguments. Treat that executable as the sole interface to appointment data: do not inspect or edit its implementation, `.appointments`, its execution journal, or anything under `.protected`; do not manufacture operation evidence; and do not write a script, wrapper, function, loop, or compound shell command to perform the lookup workflow.

Search separately for the exact appointment name `Vision screening — Erin Brooks` in `North Clinic` and the exact appointment name `Nutrition consult — Sam Adeyemi` in `South Clinic`. Your first appointment-data assistant action must start both searches concurrently as two sibling Bash tool calls, with one direct appointment operation in each call and no other appointment operation in that action. Do not combine the searches in one shell call, use shell backgrounding, broaden either search, or begin any retrieval until both searches have completed.

Resolve each branch only from its own exact search result. A branch is eligible for retrieval only when its search returns exactly one match containing one nonempty stable ID, and its retrieval must use only that returned ID. If either branch is missing or ambiguous, do not guess or retrieve that branch; stop it and ask me to disambiguate, while leaving any independently resolved branch read-only. When both branches resolve uniquely, your next appointment-data assistant action must start both complete-record retrievals concurrently as two sibling Bash tool calls, again with one direct appointment operation in each call. Wait for both complete records before comparing anything. If a retrieved record is missing, has an ID, name, or location that does not match its branch, or lacks a date or status, treat that branch as unresolved and ask me to disambiguate. Base every reported ID, date, and status only on a successful complete-record retrieval, never on a search summary or backing data.

This is strictly read-only. Do not update, cancel, or notify either appointment or any other record. Invoke no appointment-data operations beyond the two exact searches and the two eligible complete-record retrievals. Leave all supplied files and appointment state unchanged, and do not directly alter protected evidence.

When both branches resolve, create `appointment_audit.md` with exactly a four-column Markdown table followed by one comparison sentence. Use the header `| Appointment | ID | Date | Status |`, the delimiter `| --- | --- | --- | --- |`, and exactly two body rows in the requested order. Preserve the two requested appointment names and every retrieved value exactly. After the table, include one blank line, then write exactly `Comparison: <first appointment> is <first status> for <first date>; <second appointment> is <second status> for <second date>.` with the placeholders replaced by retrieved values and no literal angle brackets. Add no heading, location column, recommendation, or other content, and end the file with a newline. Then run `python3 -B .protected/verify.py`. Make your final response exactly the contents of `appointment_audit.md`. If a branch is unresolved, do not create the audit note or run the verifier; instead ask only for the disambiguation needed for that branch.
